questions.question:
Match the pairs in the following questions:

(a) Pointer data type     (p) Type conversion
(b) Activation record    (q) Dynamic data structure
(c) Repeat-until         (r) Recursion
(d) Coercion             (s) Nondeterministic loop

A. a-p, b-r, c-s, d-q
B. a-q, b-r, c-s, d-p
C. a-q, b-p, c-s, d-r
D. a-p, b-q, c-r, d-s
questions.to_find: Correct matching between compiler/runtime concepts and their descriptions

understand.plain: We need to match four compiler/runtime terms on the left with four descriptions on the right. Each term has exactly one correct description. Understanding what each term means helps us make the correct match.

keywords.explain: A block of memory allocated on the call stack when a function is called; it stores local variables, parameters, return address, and control links. It is used to implement recursion.
keywords.example: When factorial(5) calls factorial(4), a new activation record is pushed on the stack.

keywords.explain: Implicit (automatic) type conversion performed by the compiler when operands of different types are used together.
keywords.example: In C, assigning an int to a float variable is coercion; the compiler inserts a type conversion automatically.

keywords.explain: A type whose values are memory addresses. Pointers enable dynamic memory allocation, making them a vehicle for dynamic data structures like linked lists and trees.
keywords.example: int *p = malloc(sizeof(int)); — p holds a dynamically allocated address.

keywords.explain: A loop construct that executes the body at least once and checks the condition after each iteration. Without a guaranteed termination condition it can become a nondeterministic loop.
keywords.example: repeat { read(x); } until (x > 0); — may loop indefinitely if x is never positive.

understand.visual_alt: Four terms on the left (pointer data type, activation record, repeat-until, coercion) matched to four descriptions on the right (type conversion, dynamic data structure, recursion, nondeterministic loop).

given.aim: Find the correct one-to-one matching between four compiler/runtime terms and four descriptions.

terms.meaning: A type whose values are addresses; enables heap-allocated structures
Stack frame created at each function call; supports recursive calls
Post-condition loop; can loop without deterministic exit
Implicit type conversion by the compiler

given.plan: Match each term to its most specific and well-known description: pointer -> dynamic data structure; activation record -> recursion; repeat-until -> nondeterministic loop; coercion -> type conversion.

steps.title: Match Pointer data type
steps.apply: Pointers hold memory addresses and are the fundamental mechanism behind dynamic data structures (linked lists, trees, graphs). So (a) -> (q).
steps.note: Pointers enable dynamic allocation, not type conversion.

steps.title: Match Activation record
steps.apply: An activation record (stack frame) is created for every function call and is the key structure that enables recursion — each recursive call gets its own activation record. So (b) -> (r).
steps.note: Without activation records, recursive calls would overwrite each other's local state.

steps.title: Match Repeat-until
steps.apply: A repeat-until loop is a post-test loop that may never terminate if the exit condition is never satisfied, making it nondeterministic with respect to termination. So (c) -> (s).
steps.note: The nondeterministic aspect refers to uncertain loop termination.

steps.title: Match Coercion
steps.apply: Coercion is implicit (automatic) type conversion applied by the compiler when types don't match exactly. So (d) -> (p).
steps.note: Coercion differs from explicit casting; it is done automatically by the compiler.

solution.result: a-q, b-r, c-s, d-p => Option B

questions.formula_note: Compiler theory: pointer types underpin dynamic structures; activation records implement recursion; coercion is implicit type conversion.

questions.question:
State whether the following statement is TRUE or FALSE with reason:

"The Link-load-and-go loading scheme requires less storage space than the link-and-go loading scheme."

A. TRUE — Link-load-and-go does not need to store the linked output on disk before loading.
B. FALSE — Link-load-and-go requires more storage because it keeps both object code and link tables in memory simultaneously.
C. TRUE — Link-and-go requires additional table storage for symbol resolution.
D. FALSE — Both schemes use the same amount of storage.
questions.answer: B. FALSE — Link-load-and-go requires more storage because it keeps both object code and link tables in memory simultaneously.
questions.to_find: Whether link-load-and-go uses less storage space than link-and-go loading scheme

understand.plain: There are two loading schemes: 'link-and-go' and 'link-load-and-go'. We need to compare how much storage each scheme requires. The key difference is in how they handle linking and loading steps.

keywords.explain: A loading scheme where the linker produces a fully linked (but not yet loaded) program in memory, then the loader loads it. The link tables can be discarded after linking, so only the linked binary remains.
keywords.example: ld produces a.out which is then loaded into memory for execution.

keywords.explain: A scheme where linking and loading happen in a single pass simultaneously. The linking information (symbol tables, relocation data) must be kept in memory along with the partially loaded code during the entire process.
keywords.example: During execution setup, both the object modules and link tables reside in memory at the same time.

understand.visual_alt: Comparison of two loading schemes: link-and-go (link first, then load) versus link-load-and-go (both in one pass, more data in memory).

given.aim: Determine which loading scheme requires less main memory storage.

terms.meaning: Two-phase: link phase produces a binary, then load phase runs it
terms.connects: After linking, link tables are freed; only linked binary occupies memory
Both object code and link tables must coexist in memory during the pass

terms.meaning: Which scheme needs more memory at runtime
terms.example: Holding two data structures vs. one
terms.connects: Link-load-and-go holds more data simultaneously => more storage

given.plan: Analyze what data each scheme must hold in memory at the same time. Link-load-and-go must simultaneously maintain both the code and the linking data structures, requiring more memory.

steps.title: Analyze link-and-go storage
steps.apply: In link-and-go, the linker first resolves all external symbols and produces a linked output. After the linking phase completes, the linking tables (symbol tables, relocation info) can be discarded. Then the loader loads the compact linked binary. Peak memory = linked binary only.
steps.note: The link tables are temporary and are released before the program runs.

steps.title: Analyze link-load-and-go storage
steps.apply: In link-load-and-go, linking and loading occur in a single combined pass. During this pass, both the object code being loaded AND the linking information (symbol tables, external reference tables) must reside in memory simultaneously. Therefore peak memory = object code + link tables.
steps.note: The single-pass approach trades time efficiency for increased memory usage.

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.apply: Since link-load-and-go requires both code and link tables in memory at the same time, it requires MORE storage, not less. The statement is FALSE.
steps.note: The statement in the question is FALSE — link-load-and-go actually needs more storage than link-and-go.

solution.result: FALSE — Link-load-and-go requires MORE storage space than link-and-go because it must keep both object code and link tables in memory simultaneously. Option B.

questions.formula_note: Loading scheme comparison: link-and-go can discard link tables after linking; link-load-and-go must maintain them throughout, increasing memory footprint.

questions.question:
A part of the system software which under all circumstances must reside in the main memory is:

A. text editor
B. assembler
C. linker
D. loader
E. none of the above
questions.to_find: Which system software component must always reside in main memory

understand.plain: System software includes tools like editors, assemblers, linkers, and loaders. We need to identify which one must always be present in main memory. The key insight is about which tool is needed continuously at runtime versus only during development/compilation.

keywords.explain: The loader brings programs from secondary storage (disk) into main memory and prepares them for execution. At least a portion of the loader (the bootstrap loader) must always be in memory to load other programs.
keywords.example: When you run a program, the OS loader reads the executable from disk and loads it into RAM.

keywords.explain: Translates assembly language source code into machine code object files. Only needed during the build/development phase, not at runtime.
keywords.example: nasm source.asm -o source.o — runs once to produce object code.

understand.visual_alt: System software components: text editor, assembler, linker, and loader. The loader is the only one needed continuously in memory because it is responsible for bringing all other programs into memory.

given.aim: Identify which system software component cannot be swapped out and must always be in main memory.

terms.meaning: Interactive tool for writing/editing source code
terms.connects: Only needed when the user is editing; not required at runtime.
Only needed during assembly phase; not at runtime.

terms.meaning: Combines object files into a single executable
terms.connects: Only needed during the linking phase; not at runtime.

terms.meaning: Loads programs into memory for execution; part of it is always resident
terms.connects: Must always be in memory — it is the tool that loads everything else.

given.plan: Reason about when each tool is used. The loader is unique because it is needed every time a program is to be run; it cannot itself be loaded by something else unless a small resident portion always exists in memory.

steps.title: Eliminate development-only tools
steps.apply: Text editor, assembler, and linker are used only during the software development lifecycle. Once a program is compiled and linked, none of these are needed to run it. They can reside on disk and be loaded on demand.
steps.note: These tools are transient — they do not need to be permanently resident in memory.

steps.title: Analyze the loader's requirement
steps.apply: The loader is responsible for bringing executable programs from disk into main memory. For any program to run — including the assembler, linker, or editor — the loader must first load it. Therefore, at least a resident portion of the loader must always be in memory. Without it, no program could be loaded.
steps.note: In modern systems this is part of the OS kernel; classically, the bootstrap loader is hardwired or in ROM.

solution.result: The loader (option D) must always reside in main memory because it is responsible for loading all other programs.

questions.formula_note: Fundamental OS/loader principle: the loader must be resident in memory at all times to bootstrap and load any other software component.

questions.question:
A linker is given object modules for a set of programs that were compiled separately. What information needs to be included in an object module?

A. Object code
B. Relocation bits
C. Names and locations of all external symbols defined in the object module
D. Absolute addresses of internal symbols
questions.to_find: Which pieces of information must be present in an object module for a linker to function

understand.plain: When a compiler compiles a source file, it produces an object module. The linker then takes multiple object modules and combines them into one executable. For the linker to do its job, each object module must contain certain information. We need to identify what is necessary.

keywords.explain: The machine instructions and data produced by the compiler from source code. This is the primary content of an object module.
keywords.example: Compiled binary instructions for a function like printf().

keywords.explain: Flags or tables indicating which addresses in the object code are relative/relocatable and need to be adjusted when the module is placed at a final memory address.
keywords.example: A jump instruction target address that must be updated when code is relocated.

keywords.explain: A table listing the names and relative offsets/locations of all symbols (functions, variables) defined in this module that other modules can reference.
keywords.example: A module defining function 'sort' exports its name and offset so other modules can call it.

keywords.explain: Fixed, non-relocatable memory addresses. Object modules use relative/relocatable addresses, not absolute ones, because the final load address is not known at compile time.
keywords.example: An absolute address like 0x4000 in an object module would be incorrect since the module may be loaded anywhere.

understand.visual_alt: Object module structure: object code (main content), relocation bits (which addresses to fix up), and external symbol table (exported names). Absolute addresses are NOT included since load address is unknown at compile time.

given.aim: Identify which information an object module must contain for the linker to combine it with other modules.

terms.meaning: Machine code instructions produced by the compiler
terms.connects: Essential — the actual program instructions that will execute.

terms.meaning: Metadata identifying which addresses in the code need patching
terms.example: Flags on address fields in branch/call instructions
terms.connects: Needed by the linker and loader to fix up addresses when placing the module at its final location.

terms.meaning: Export table: symbols defined here and usable by other modules
terms.connects: Needed by the linker to resolve cross-module references.
NOT included — object modules use position-independent/relocatable addresses; absolute addresses can only be assigned after the final load address is determined.

given.plan: Object modules must contain the actual code (A), relocation information so the linker/loader can fix up addresses (B), and an export symbol table so the linker can resolve cross-module references (C). Absolute addresses (D) are not used because object modules are position-independent.

steps.title: Object code is mandatory
steps.apply: Without the compiled machine instructions, the object module has no content. Object code (A) is always included.
steps.note: This is the primary payload of any object module.

steps.title: Relocation bits are mandatory
steps.apply: Since object modules are compiled without knowledge of their final load address, addresses embedded in instructions are relative. Relocation bits (B) tell the linker/loader which fields must be adjusted. Without them, address fixup is impossible.
steps.note: Every relocatable object module contains a relocation table.

steps.title: External symbol information is mandatory
steps.apply: When module X calls function 'sort' defined in module Y, the linker must know that module Y exports 'sort' at some offset. So the external symbol table (C) — listing all exported names and their locations — is mandatory.
steps.note: This is the basis for the linker's symbol resolution step.

steps.title: Absolute addresses are NOT included
steps.apply: Object modules do not contain absolute addresses (D) because the final memory location is not known at compile time. Using absolute addresses would make the module non-relocatable. The linker assigns absolute addresses only when producing the final executable.
steps.note: Using absolute addresses in object modules would break relocation.

solution.result: A, B, and C must be included in an object module. D (absolute addresses of internal symbols) is NOT included.

questions.formula_note: Linker operation: requires object code, relocation bits, and export symbol tables; absolute addresses are assigned only at final link/load time.

questions.question:
The correct matching for the following pairs is:

(A) Activation record     (1) Linking loader
(B) Location counter      (2) Garbage collection
(C) Reference counts      (3) Subroutine call
(D) Address relocation    (4) Assembler

A. A-3, B-4, C-1, D-2
B. A-4, B-3, C-1, D-2
C. A-4, B-3, C-2, D-1
D. A-3, B-4, C-2, D-1
questions.to_find: Correct matching between four runtime/system concepts and their associated tools or operations

understand.plain: We must match four compiler/system software terms on the left with the tool or operation they are most closely associated with on the right. Each term has a unique best match.

keywords.explain: A stack frame created when a subroutine (function) is called; stores local variables, return address, parameters.
keywords.example: Calling factorial(n) creates an activation record for that call.

keywords.explain: A counter maintained by the assembler that tracks the current address (offset) being assigned to each instruction or data item during assembly.
keywords.example: After assembling a 3-byte instruction, the location counter increments by 3.

keywords.explain: A memory management technique where each object tracks how many pointers refer to it; when the count drops to zero, the object is eligible for garbage collection.
keywords.example: Python uses reference counting to manage memory automatically.

keywords.explain: The process of adjusting addresses in loaded code to reflect the actual memory location where the program was loaded. Performed by the linking loader.
keywords.example: A program compiled for address 0x0 loaded at 0x1000 needs all addresses incremented by 0x1000.

understand.visual_alt: Four terms matched to operations: Activation record -> Subroutine call; Location counter -> Assembler; Reference counts -> Garbage collection; Address relocation -> Linking loader.

given.aim: Determine the correct one-to-one mapping between four system software/runtime concepts and their primary associated operations or tools.

terms.meaning: Stack frame for a function call

terms.example: Assembler increments it after each instruction

terms.meaning: Count of pointers to a heap object for memory management
Adjusting addresses to reflect actual load location
terms.example: Relocation table processed at load time

given.plan: Match each term to its primary tool/operation: activation record -> subroutine call; location counter -> assembler; reference counts -> garbage collection; address relocation -> linking loader.

steps.title: Match Activation record
steps.apply: An activation record is created on the stack whenever a subroutine is called. It stores the local context for that call. The primary association is with subroutine call (3). So A -> 3.
steps.note: Every subroutine call triggers allocation of a new activation record.

steps.title: Match Location counter
steps.apply: The location counter is an internal counter used by the assembler to assign addresses to symbols and instructions as it processes the source. It is a fundamental part of how an assembler works. So B -> 4 (Assembler).
steps.note: The location counter is what allows the assembler to compute the address of each label.

steps.title: Match Reference counts
steps.apply: Reference counting is a well-known garbage collection strategy. Each heap object maintains a count of live references; when the count hits zero, the memory is reclaimed. So C -> 2 (Garbage collection).
steps.note: Reference counting is one approach to automatic memory management (along with mark-and-sweep).

steps.title: Match Address relocation
steps.apply: Address relocation is the process of fixing up code addresses after the program is placed at its actual memory location. This is done by the linking loader, which combines linking (resolving symbols) and loading (placing in memory) with address fixup. So D -> 1 (Linking loader).
steps.note: A linking loader both links external references and relocates addresses in a single step.

steps.title: Select matching option
steps.apply: A-3, B-4, C-2, D-1 corresponds to option D.
steps.note: Verify against all options: only option D gives A-3, B-4, C-2, D-1.

solution.result: A-3, B-4, C-2, D-1 => Option D

questions.formula_note: System software associations: activation records are for subroutine calls; location counter is an assembler construct; reference counting is a garbage collection method; address relocation is performed by the linking loader.
